text.title: Glossary Management — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that keep a project glossary alive instead of letting it decay into yet-another-Confluence-page.

rule.statement: The project has EXACTLY ONE glossary location. Every PRD, spec, ADR, test plan, and code constants file MUST link to terms here. Forbidden: parallel mini-glossaries inside individual documents.

rationale.source: DDD ubiquitous-language practice, Google TW glossary guide

rule.rationale: Parallel glossaries diverge within a quarter. Single source forces conflict resolution to happen once, in public. Links from docs to glossary entries also make the glossary's traffic measurable — low traffic flags terms nobody actually uses.

rule.statement: Each entry MUST declare its scope: project-wide OR a named bounded context (e.g., "billing", "auth", "marketing-funnel"). A term can have different definitions per scope, but each scope's definition is explicit.
rule.rationale: "Customer" means different things to billing and to support. Forcing scope makes the difference explicit; without scope, teams argue about which definition is the "right" one when both are right in their context.

rule.statement: When two stakeholders disagree about a definition, follow the protocol: (a) document both definitions verbatim, (b) maintainer schedules a 30-min resolution session, (c) outcome is one of: pick winner / split into two scoped terms / merge into broader term, (d) record decision + date + named participants in conflict-resolution-record.md.
rule.rationale: Without protocol, conflict resolves by whichever stakeholder is louder, leaving the loser's definition surviving in shadow docs. Protocol creates a single explicit outcome that can be referenced later.

rule.statement: Terms are NOT deleted. Deprecation sets status=deprecated, adds a replaced_by link to the new canonical term, and triggers term-usage-audit. The deprecated entry stays in the glossary indefinitely so historical docs remain interpretable.
rule.rationale: Deletion breaks historical docs. Deprecation with replacement preserves interpretability while signaling new work to use the new term. Status flag is what audit script keys off to drive cleanup.

rule.statement: Every term entry MUST list its propagation hooks: where this term appears (PRD section, code module / constant, test plan ID, support macro, marketing copy). When the entry changes, the maintainer notifies hook owners. Forbidden: glossary entries without any propagation hook (term that lives nowhere is term that should be deleted).
rule.rationale: Propagation hooks make glossary changes actionable. Without them, the BA changes a definition and nobody updates the code constant, leaving a silent drift. Term-usage audit operationalizes this.

example.title: Scoped term with conflict resolution + propagation hooks
example.code:
Term: "Active Customer"
Scope: billing Status: canonical Definition: A customer account whose subscription is in {trialing, active, past_due} states AND last_login < 30 days ago. Aliases: "engaged customer" (deprecated; replaced_by Active Customer) Conflicts-with: support team's "active" (any account not deleted) -- resolved 2026-03-12: split into two scoped terms. See conflict-resolution-records/2026-03-12-active.md Propagation hooks: - PRD section: /docs/billing/prd.md#segments - Code constant: billing.constants.ACTIVE_CUSTOMER_STATES - Test plan: TP-billing-014 - Support macro: SM-renewal-001 Last reviewed: 2026-05-12 by alex.k (BA)
example.note: Illustrates r1-r5: single canonical entry, scoped, conflict resolved on record, deprecation linked, hooks listed.

example.title: Term sprawl across docs
example.code:
Confluence "customer" page: vague definition, last edited 14 months ago. Billing PRD: defines "customer" inline, contradicts Confluence. Support macros: use "active customer" with a third definition. Code: customer_segment table has 4 enum values not in any of the above.
Result: bug "customer not receiving renewal notice" debated 90 min in a Slack thread because three parties had three customers in mind.
Repair: enforce r1-r5. Move all definitions to single glossary, split into scopes, run propagation audit, deprecate the rest.
example.note: Illustrates r1, r2, r5 violations — the standard BA pain point.
